_Zhenlong Wang[1] , Zhigang Li[1] , Zhipeng Xia[1] , Xiao Sun[1] , Jialin Meng[1,2,4*] , and Tianyu Wang[1,2,3*  ]_

_1School of Integrated Circuits, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China; Suzhou Research Institute of Shandong University, Suzhou, 215123, China_

_2National Integrated Circuit Innovation Center, Shanghai 201203, China;_

_3 State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China; 4Key Laboratory of Computational Neuroscience and Brain-Inspired Intelligence (Fudan University), Ministry of Education, Shanghai 200433, P. R. China *Email: jlmeng@sdu.edu.cn; tywang@sdu.edu.cn_ **ABSTRACT** Memristor-based in-memory computing technology represents a paradigm shift in data processing and storage by integrating computation and memory functionalities within a single element, showing great potential in artificial intelligence, big data analytics, and Internet of Things (IoT) fields. Complementary metal-oxidesemiconductor (CMOS) compatible oxide memristors, which leverage mature atomic layer deposition (ALD) and physical vapor deposition (PVD) processes, exhibit unparalleled advantages in neuromorphic computing applications. They can be seamlessly integrated with existing CMOS logic circuits through back-end-of-line (BEOL) processes, enabling monolithic 3D stacking for ultra-high density synaptic arrays; meanwhile, their ultralow power consumption (<10 fJ/switch) and nanosecondscale switching speeds outperform biological synaptic dynamics. Additionally, oxygen vacancy engineering endows them with multistate storage capabilities, which is essential for high-precision neural network training, and their self-rectifying characteristics can suppress sneak currents in crossbar architectures without the need for additional selectors. This paper discusses oxide-based memristors from aspects of materials systems, device mechanisms, integrated arrays, and intelligent applications. The roles of memristors in memory, logical operations, and neuromorphic computing

are systematically analyzed. Finally, challenges and prospects of CMOS-compatible oxide memristors are discussed for next-generation brain-inspired computing systems.

Mohamed Radwan,* Seyed Hossein Hosseini-Shokouh,* Abde Mayeen Shafi, Catarina Dias, Fooqia Khalid, Shreya Rajeevan, Henri Hyttinen, Fida Ali, Faisal Ahmed, Zhipei Sun, and Harri Lipsanen*

**Cite This:** _Nano Lett._ 2025, 25, 17970−17977

**Read Online**

ACCESS Metrics & More Article Recommendations * **sı** Supporting Information

ABSTRACT: Neuro-based computing, enabled by memristors and memtransistors, has drawn significant attention, with twodimensional (2D) molybdenum ditelluride (MoTe2) emerging as a promising candidate for its low phase-change energy barrier and tunable electrical behavior. However, realizing the full potential of MoTe2 for neuromorphic applications has been hindered by the lack of a sufficiently large memory window in pristine devices. In this work, we present an effective method to significantly enhance the analogue switching of lateral MoTe2 devices. This method involves two sequential processes: laser treatment followed by the atomic layer deposition of Al2O3. The synergistic effects of both processes yielded significant performance improvements, with a 70-fold improvement in dynamic range when operating as a

**==> picture [241 x 133] intentionally omitted <==**

memristor and a 20-fold enhancement when functioning as a memtransistor. Consequently, artificial neural network simulations demonstrated a 10-fold enhancement in pattern recognition accuracy, while the dual memristor/memtransistor capability enabled the emulation of both homosynaptic and heterosynaptic plasticity.

KEYWORDS: _Memristor, Memtransistor, Neuromorphic, 2D Materials, Analogue Switching, Laser Patterning, Atomic Layer Deposition_

I intelligencen recent years,(AI), thethe rapidInternetadvancementof Things of(IoT),artificialand machine learning has led to a sharp rise in computing demands, significantly increasing energy consumption and the amount of processed data. To address these challenges, neuromorphic computing, utilizing brain-inspired devices, has gained attention as a promising solution. In particular, memristors and memtransistors, which mimic synaptic behavior, exhibit low power consumption, high integration density, and brain-like operational capabilities, making them ideal nanodevices for emerging neuromorphic computing.[1][−][6]

A wide range of materials have been investigated to build memristors and memtransistors�from traditional oxide materials[7][,][8] to new materials such as perovskite, organic, ferroelectric, and two-dimensional (2D) materials.[9][−][12] In particular, 2D materials have drawn significant attention for their flexibility, excellent electrical properties, strong gate tunability, and atomically thin nature, making them a compelling choice for neuromorphic devices.[13][−][15]

Owing to their tunable energy bandgap and significant surface-to-volume ratio, 2D materials offer a high degree of freedom for device optimization through postprocessing methods.[16][−][21] Among 2D materials, molybdenum ditelluride (MoTe2) offers distinct advantages, including a significantly

lower phase change energy barrier,[21][,][22] a bandgap closely matching the bandgap of silicon,[23] and tunable electrical behavior.[24] However, utilizing MoTe2’s full potential for memory applications remains challenging due to the limited memory window and dynamic range (DR) in pristine devices.

J.J.Y. and R.Z. conceived the concept and designed the experiments. R.Z. and T.M. fabricated the devices with the help of Y.Z. and S.A. J.Z., S.J.K., H.-T.L., C.B. and K.M. helped with the material characterizations. J.-W.H. provided the customized transistors. R.Z. conducted all the electrical measurements with the help of Y.Z. and R.M. R.Z. and T.W. designed and carried out 1M1T1R neuron and neural network simulation. R.Z. and P.S. did the energy and area efficiency estimation. R.Z. and Y.X. did the literature search on artificial neurons. R.Z., T.W., D.G., Z.R., Q.W., S.G., A.K.R., Q.Q., R.S.W. and Y.C. analysed the data and results. J.J.Y. and R.Z. wrote the paper. R.S.W., P.S. and T.W. helped to improve the paper. All authors discussed the results and implications and commented on the paper.

Very recently, Gia et al. demonstrated an FeTJ based on an ultra-thin BSO film of thickness 4.6 nm deposited on Nb:STO using a chemical solution method (Fig. 7(a)).[34] The FeTJ exhibited long retention time (10 years, Fig. 7(b)) and exceptional cycling endurance (10[9] ). The stable tetragonal-like phase induced by Sm substitution provided prolonged retention in the BSO-based FeTJs by reducing the depolarization field. Furthermore, the BSO film showed an excellent Pr of 50 mC cm[�][2] and a very low Ec of 0.75 MV cm[�][1] . Moreover, the FeTJ displayed a large tunnelling electroresistance compared to other materials attributable to the large polarization maintained at the minute thickness (Fig. 7(c)). Consequently, both the ferroelectric barrier height and barrier width at the depletion region surface of Nb:STO were modulated. In this work, a single BSO-based FeTJ demonstrated 32 conductance states without any write-termination process. Hence, this new material warrants further deeper explorative studies of its neuromorphic behavior.

A summary comparing Pr, Ec, endurance, retention, and write energy of the different materials, together with neuromorphic performance parameters, is shown in Table 3. It is possible to conclude that Hf0.5Zr0.5O2 partially meets the requirements of neuromorphic applications. In the case of Hf0.5Zr0.5O2, we took the best performance[56] from Fig. 5 to compare the neuromorphic parameters with other materials. However, these novel ferroelectrics are in the early stage of development and strong performance improvements are eagerly anticipated.

_Perovskite_ : Perovskite materials show great potential for the fabrication of memory devices with multiple sensory responses due to their diverse functionalities such as ferroelectric, piezoelectric, thermoelectric, and photosensitive.[[168]] The chemical structure of perovskites is usually shown as ABX3, where A represents cations, which can be organic cations like methylammonium/formamidine (MA/FA) or inorganic cations like Ca, Bi, Cs; the B represents metal atom, usually Pb and Sn; X can be oxygen or halide ions. It usually consists of BX6 octahedra, which determines the optoelectronic properties such as absorption coefficient and charge transport. Among them, perovskite oxide and perovskite halide have been widely employed as the switching layer of memristors with a high on/off ratio, flexibility, and multilevel property, and applicability to artificial synapses/neurons.[[169]] **Table 3** lists some volatile memristors based on different perovskites in representative studies.

Oxygen deficiency and substitutional donor doping are reliable pathways to improve and modulate the performance of perovskite memristors.[[170]] The oxygen-rich stoichiometry of perovskite oxide materials enables the memristor with rich RS behaviors to meet the demands of ANNs.[[171]] Sriram and coworkers examined the origins of switching performance in Nbdoped amorphous SrTiO3− _x_ memristor devices, and highlighted the utility of donor dopants to modulate volatile and non-volatile behavior in SrTiO3 devices.[[172]] Liu and co-workers reported the stable TS behavior in the Au/Cr/BaTiO3/Nb: SrTiO3/In devices which was attributed to the formation/rupture of the oxygen vacancy CFs by varying the compliance current and temperature.[[173]] Ramanathan and co-workers reported that hydrogen doped NdNiO3 devices could be electrically reconfigured on demand to emulate neurons, synapses, or memory capacitor functions ( **Figure 5** a).[[174]] By annealing NdNiO3 devices in hydrogen atmosphere (with catalytically active electrodes such as Pd or Pt), hydrogen inserted into the lattice of NdNiO3 closed to the electrode. The doped hydrogens act as electron donors, modifying the orbital configuration of Ni and thus leading to a decrease in the conductivity of NdNiO3. Multitude of electronic states was then facilitated by the synergistic redistributing protons doped in the perovskite lattice under different electric fields.

In place of oxide anions, perovskite halides provide more families with improved semiconducting properties,[[175]] tunable

Memristors were made using the in vacuo ALD deposition described by Goul et al in their recent work [34]. Memristors were deposited on an Si/SiO2 wafer chip in an interconnected sputtering and ALD system in order to minimize exposure to atmosphere during the fabrication process. The memristor Al and Pd electrodes were deposited using a DC magnetron sputter at a base pressure of <6.0 × 10[−][7] Torr, using an Ar plasma (14 mTorr/ 90 W for Al and 30 mTorr/45 W for Pd). After the 10 nm thick bottom Al electrode was deposited, the wafer chip was transferred to the ALD chamber. The ALD chamber had been pre-heated to 225 °C and the chip was allowed to dynamically heat for 25 min before ALD deposition of the memristor active layer. The active layer was made by depositing a total of 17 ALD layers composed of a mixture of Al2O3 and MgO atomic layers, using trimethylaluminum or bis(cyclopentadienyl)magnesium(II), respectively, as the primary precursor. The primary precursor was injected into the ALD chamber for 2 s to deposit the Al2O3 or MgO and was followed by a 35 s N2 purge. A 2 s pulse of deionized H2O was introduced to the ALD chamber and followed by an N2 purge for 35 s. The sample was removed from the ALD chamber and cooled to room temperature under vacuum following the ALD deposition. Vacuum was broken to exchange the shadow mask for the bottom electrode with the shadow mask for the top electrode. The top electrode was sputtered onto the memristor by depositing 25 nm of Pd onto the sample. The resulting memristors are 200 × 200 μm[2] . It should be noted that the lateral dimensions of memristors fabricated using shadow masks are typically several orders of magnitude larger than those of memristors fabricated using microfabrication techniques such as photolithography and e-beam lithography [40–43]. The effect the larger device footprint has on the device properties is principally observed in the device HRS value since the conduction mechanism for the HRS (Poole–Frenkel emission, space-charge-limited conduction, hopping conduction, etc) are proportional to the cross-sectional area of the device. When the memristor is in the LRS, the current flow is dependent on the cross-sectional area of the CF rather than the cross-

Various preparation methods for 2D materials have been developed since the discovery of graphene in 2004: mechanical exfoliation [110–113], chemical exfoliation and intercalation [114, 115], and chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [116–122].

Mechanical exfoliation is the most convenient method to obtain 2D monolayers in the laboratory [123–125]. The quality, purity, and cleanness of the 2D materials prepared by the mechanical exfoliation method are found to be the best among 2D materials by various fabrication methods. However, producing large-area monolayers by the mechanical exfoliation is difficult [126]; the mechanical exfoliation method is not scalable and time-consuming, which makes it difficult for the systematic control of the thickness and lateral

Chemical methods including chemical exfoliation and intercalation could boost the production yield of atomically thin flakes. However, the quality of the 2D materials prepared by the chemical methods is low; external ions or compounds are inevitably involved in the chemical exfoliation/intercalation processes. In

a-GaOx thin films are deposited by RF magnetron sputtering in Technol Magnetron Sputtering System JCP500. The high-purity sintered gallium oxide disk with 2.0 in. diameter (ZhongNuo Advanced Material Inc.) is used as a target, which is 10.5 cm from the substrate. Quartz, silicon, and ITO are used simultaneously as substrates for device preparation and a-GaOx characterization. The three kinds of substrates are cleaned by acetone, ethanol, de-ionized water, and being dried up by nitrogen before film deposition. The chamber is pumped down to a base pressure of 8 � 10[�][4] Pa, and Ar pressure is at 1.3 Pa with 100 SCCM for a-GaOx deposition. The sputtering power is set at 30 W. The target is pre-sputtered for 5 min, and then 5 and 7 nm a-GaOx are obtained at ambient temperature after sputtering for 12 and 16 min, respectively. Subsequently, a thinner WOx layer with high VO content is deposited on a-GaOx/ITO substrates by HWCVD, and then W/a-GaOx/ITO and W/WOx/a-GaOx/ITO memristors are finished by depositing W electrodes using HWCVD through the mask with 0.4 mm-diameter pores, as reported in our previous articles.[39][,][40] D-1 to D-4 are designated to the four memristors with different structures and different a-GaOx thicknesses as listed in Table I.

The current–voltage (I–V) curves of four memristors are measured by Keithley 2635A, in which W electrode is biased and ITO electrode is grounded. a-GaOx are characterized as follows: The textures are obtained by grazing incidence x-ray diffraction (GIXRD), the chemical element compositions are analyzed by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha), and the bandgap is calculated from the transmission spectrum in 200–1100 nm (MAYA 2000 Pro, Ocean Optics). Additionally, the cross section of a W/WOx/a-GaOx/ITO is characterized by SEM (HITACHI SU8010) with EDS (energy dispersive spectrometer) energy spectrum analysis instrument.

For the in situ solvothermal deposition of Sb2S3 films from [Sb{S2P{O(Pr)2}3], a dispersion of [Sb{S2P{O(Pr)2}3] (1 g) in 20 mL of ethylene glycol was prepared and stirred for 1 h. Film deposition on a cleaned fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) substrate was carried out in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 180 °C for 24 hours. The resulting black film was then vacuumdried for 24 hours, completing the in situ solvothermal process for fabricating Sb2S3 films, as illustrated in Figure 5.12. The in situ solvothermal process serve was utilised as an alternative low-temperature solution-processed method. During the deposition, the FTO substrate was immersed in a solution of [Sb{S2P{O(Pr)2}3]. Ethylene glycol allows the achievement of a low-temperature thermal process and avoids any further annealing process. An appropriate solvent environment plays an important role in protecting film from oxidation. The solvent

Se 3d, and Al 2p, and O1s are sequentially presented and analyzed in Figs. 1c–1f. All core-level XPS spectra were fitted with the Gaussian– Lorentzian (5%) function using XPSPEAK4.1 fitting software after applying the Shirley background function. In Fig. 1c, the deconvoluted peaks due to the spin–orbit doublets at 336.45 and 341.7 eV are ascribed to Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, respectively. Further, peaks appeared around 337.6 and 342.85 eV owing to the oxidized states of PdOx, indicating interfacial reaction near Al2O3/PdSe2. The doublet peaks in Fig. 1d at 54.5 and 55.35 eV are attributed to Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2 respectively. Oxidized spin–orbit splitting of the Se 3d core-level states of SeOx was found at 56.7 eV and 58.6 eV with reduced intensity. The diffusion of Se atoms, which react with Al2O3, is the origin of intrinsic Se vacancy defects inside the PdSe2 dendrites that influence the resistive switching in the memristor. The core-level Pd and Se states appeared because of the presence of 2D layered PdSe2; similar results have been confirmed by Li _et al_ . and Jena _et al_ . in their previous experiments [1,27]. The presence of Al2O3 layers was confirmed by the Al 2p core level at a binding energy of 74.25 eV shown in Fig. 1e. The satellite peak located at 72.4 eV –O–Pd and Al–O– confirms a mixed bonding of Al Se. To confirm the interfacial oxidations, O 1 s core-level high-resolution XPS spectra were fitted in Fig. 1f with three peaks denoted as O(I), O(II), and O(III). The – peak at 530.8 eV related to Al O emerged owing to the presence of top and bottom ALD-Al2O3 layers. Meanwhile, the other two small-intensity peaks at 532.4 and 534.1 eV are related to the interface layer formation after high-temperature ALD of Al2O3 on PdSe2 and are related to Al–O–Pd/Se.

Considering all the respective data, it can be concluded that the achieved memristor is highly stable under normal conditions, and the oxygen vacancies can alter via oxygen pulses during ALD deposition.

For comparison, we also inserted thicker Cr-layers (the I–V characteristics are given in figure S6) as an adhesive Pt/Cr (7.5 nm)/TiOx/Au (the I–V characteristics are given in figure S6a) and Pt/Cr (10 nm)/TiOx/Au (the I–V characteristics is shown in figure S6b), respectively. The results demonstrated switching at the negative bias, and after switching once, they did not switch back at both positive and negative voltages, instead following a straight resistor-like path (shown by red arrows in the I–V curve in figure S6). It can also be seen that as the Cr thickness increases from 7.5 nm to 10 nm, the switching voltage also increases. We then fabricated devices with Cr functioning as the TE (the I–V characteristics are given in figure S7). The I–V characteristics showed switching at −2.05 V, allowing 695 microamps of current to flow. Identical switching was observed (like Cr 7.5 nm and Cr10 nm); once the device is switched on, it follows the write-once read many times (WORM) structure and does not switch on or off. In addition, we fabricated a device architecture (Pt/Cr

(2.5 nm)/TiOx/Au) (given in figure S8) with Cr having a thickness of 2.5 nm. The electrical characterization of the memristive cell revealed that the devices did not switch on the positive voltage but switched at the negative voltage (identical switching to the above-mentioned devices except the switching voltage), and after switching once, it did not switch back at both positive and negative voltage, instead following a straight resistor-like path (shown by red arrows).

To elucidate the chemical dynamics during RS behavior in a TiOx-based unipolar volatile RS memristor, the double logarithmic I–V characteristics were plotted to describe the nature of conduction. Figure 3 illustrates the RS between HRS and LRS, figure 3(a) shows the RS switching mechanism from the Pt electrode to Au as the grounded electrode, and figure 3(b) demonstrates the RS switching mechanism from the Au electrode to the Pt (grounded) electrode. The RS mechanistic approach behind the RS switching has not been clarified completely; however, the hypothesis regarding TiOx devices reveals that switching at room temperature is mainly dominated by oxygen vacancies (Vox) in the switching layer [39]. Generally, the intrinsic Vox in the nonconductive TiOx layer assists the growth of the conductive filament. Initially, the memristor occupied the HRS owing to the

The high-speed (O-poor) and low-speed (O-rich) memristors exhibit low nonlinearity coefficients of 0.52/1.22 and 0.54/0.91, respectively. In the process of updating the weight at the neural network, the linearity of the devices in the array directly determines the weight mapping accuracy [27], and the high linearity LTDP characteristics of both high-speed and low-speed devices make it very suitable for application in neural networks.

The conductance update ratio of the high-speed memristor is larger than that of the low-speed one. A combined unit is proposed using the high-speed and low-speed memristor, which corresponds to three modes (mixed, high-speed only, and low-speed only). The O-poor devices are adopted coarse updates to accelerate the training process, while O-rich devices are used for fine updates to improve accuracy. The O-rich device can correct the internal interferences caused by the O-poor ones during the coarse updates process with large step. With a typical DNN structure with input (784 nodes),

**==> picture [235 x 175] intentionally omitted <==**

Fig. 5. (a) Schematic of dynamic hidden layer in DNN. (b) _N_ hidden and SNR mapping for classification accuracy with the guideline of 80% (black) and 70% (red). (c) Accuracy under external interference with fixed and dynamic hidden layers.

hidden (100 nodes), and output (ten nodes) layers, Fig. 4(b) displays the confusion matrices by using mixed mode based on the Fashion-MNIST dataset. Fig. 4(c) shows the error rate of the combined units with three modes, in which a maximum recognition accuracy of 83.15% is achieved in mixed mode. Experimental results indicate the mixed mode outperforms single device configurations in both operating speed and recognition accuracy. Furthermore, the impacts of internal disturbances on accuracy are shown in Fig. 4(d) with a variation from 0% to 50%. The mixed mode shows better robustness compared to the other two modes, which is due to low-speed devices can compensate for update errors caused by the high-speed devices.

For Artificial Intelligence (AI) tasks, the training and inference processes are influenced not only by internal interference but also by external disturbances such as Gaussian white noise. To enhance the resistance to external interference, a dynamic hidden layer strategy is proposed without significantly increasing the network size. The illustrated image of the dynamic hidden layer is displayed in Fig. 5(a), corresponding to two weight layers (W1 and W2). The W2 layer is implemented by using the combined units. The principle involves comparing the received noisy images with the original images to obtain the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Based on the SNR and threshold recognition accuracy (Acc1), the nodes in the dynamic hidden layer are gradually adjusted for minimal redundancy and maximum accuracy. The enlarged network can avoid external interference to a certain degree.

The mapping information between node number in the hidden layer and noise immunity is shown in Fig. 5(b) with the red guide line of 70% accuracy and the black guide line of 80%. Fig. 5(c) shows the relationship between accuracy and SNR with given (25 and 150) and dynamic (from 25 to 150) node numbers. Compared to a fixed hidden layer (Nh = 150), the average network size is reduced by 47%, demonstrating an energy-efficiency balance performance.

Figure S2 illustrates the physical schematic of the writing and erasing process in this GO RRAM. First, GO is in a pristine state where no conductive filament is formed. Then, by application of a positive voltage to the TE, the GO functional groups detach from GO sheets and move toward TE. This phenomenon leaves several electron trappings sites on the GO sheets, which form a conductive filament from BE to TE. As the negative voltage polarization is applied, the functional groups move back to their original bindings and the device resistance increases due to the reduction of electron trapping sites.[53][,][64] It has been found that the switching operation of GO resistive switching memory (RRAM) is governed by a dual mechanism of desorption/adsorption of oxygen-related groups and formation/rupture of metal filaments in the GO film. Some papers[53] mentioned that oxygen migration is the main mechanism for switching operation in GO RRAM. As well − known, a GO layer consists of epoxide, hydroxyl (C OH), and carboxyl (C−O) groups. The resistive switching in the Al/ GO/ITO device may be due to the desorption/absorption of these groups near the top electrode (Al). The existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the GO surface leads to high resistance (HRS) of the memory cell due to sp[3] bonding. When a positive voltage is applied to the Al top electrode, oxygen-related functional groups near Al inside the GO layer are removed and sp[2] bonds are created. Therefore, the concentration of interlayer electrons increases and the device is switched to the LRS. When the polarity of the voltage changes to a negative value, the oxygen-functional groups attach to GO sheets and the memory cell returns to HRS. The formation/ rupture of metal filaments is another possible mechanism that is the effect of the Al top electrode. In the LRS, a conducting

filament is created due to the diffusion of Al into the GO layer. On the other hand, in the HRS, Al−O is detected in the switching mechanism, but no Al is detected.

**4.3. Multilevel Resistive Switching.** The multilevel switching potential of GO RRAM was explored by applying different compliance currents, as illustrated in Figure 7. As shown in Figure 7a, some LRS and HRS levels were obtained by adjusting the compliance current from 5 to 9 mA. Hence, higher compliance currents induced higher storage levels. As shown in Figure 7b, the LRS resistance decreased from 610 to 480 Ω at 1 V reading voltage as a result of current compliance reduction.

Oxide-based optically controlled synaptic devices have drawn huge attention for non-volatile memory switching, neuromorphic applications for faster switching, and CMOS compatibility. Future development of optoelectric-based synaptic devices

To enhance the storage performance of volatile memristors while enabling multifunctional applications, material system innovation and structural optimization are crucial. The presence and removal of external bias on the electrodes will result in the connection and breakage of the conducting filaments formed by the oxygen vacancies within the insulating layer of the memristor,[12] leaving the device to exhibit a resistive switching (RS) behavior between the high resistance state (HRS) and the low resistance state (LRS). This RS phenomenon is the basis of information storage in the memristor. The inherently low activation energy of the organic−inorganic hybrid material permits rapid ion migration,[13] and the high surface diffusion coefficient facilitates the modulation of the volatility of the conducting filaments.[14] However, the higher current density of single-layer organic− inorganic hybrid-based memristor based on the on-state leads to poor device endurance (about 100 cycles), which is attributed to localized Joule heating and poor heat − dissipation.[15] Meanwhile, existing organic inorganic hybrid material fabrication processes such as the sol−gel method[16] are

incompatible with CMOS processes. Therefore, from stability considerations, designing a bilayer stack structure can effectively control the concentration gradient of oxygen vacancies and ensure the stable formation and breakage of conductive filaments, which improves the endurance and reproducibility of the device.[17][,][18] From a compatibility perspective, the use of atomic layer deposition (ALD)[19] with self-limiting and self-saturating characteristics benefits the deposition of thin films with excellent uniformity over large areas and complex three-dimensional surface conformality. As a branch of ALD, molecular layer deposition (MLD) allows the use of organic molecules as precursors to deposit polymers or organic−inorganic hybrid materials as functional layers in a memristor.[20] Nevertheless, the study on MLD organic− inorganic hybrid thin film for a volatile memristor remains rather scarce.

In this work, a multifunctional volatile memristor based on organic−inorganic hybrid thin films was explored by MLD/ ALD for neuromorphic computing and artificial intelligence applications. The ultrathin bilayer device of TiN/6 nm titanium-based maleic acid (Ti-MA)/4 nm Al2O3/Pt (TTAP) has been constructed to achieve bipolar resistive switching behavior with better endurance, lower set/reset energy consumption, and tuning data retention loss. The related mechanism has been proposed based on the conductive filament model caused by the oxygen vacancies migration/ diffusion. Some important biosynaptic activities have been emulated in single TTAP memristor, such as long-term potentiation (LTP), long-term depression (LTD), paired pulse facilitation (PPF), post-tetanic potentiation (PTP),

Ferroelectric memristor, which employ ferroelectric materials, have been under research since the 1900s [18,19]. Initially, these studies focused on perovskite materials. However, the use of perovskite materials posed significant challenges, particularly in terms of compatibility with CMOS technology and the difficulty of utilizing them in thin film applications [20,21]. To overcome these issues, hafnium oxide (HfO₂) has emerged as a promising alternative. Hafnium oxide not only addresses the compatibility concerns with existing semiconductor technology but also offers the potential for integration into thin film structures [22–24]. Consequently, the adoption of hafnium oxide in ferroelectric memristor devices paves the way for more practical and scalable neuromorphic computing solutions. To achieve high-density storage, this study introduces a CBA ferroelectric memristor device

capable of replicating the functions of artificial synapses, thereby enabling multifunctional behaviors illustrated in Fig. 1. To emulate the operations of the biological brain, we tested various synapse functions in the ferroelectric memristor device using electrical experiments. Additionally, we investigated the depolarization field present within the ferroelectric memristor device and demonstrated that the resulting retention loss can be transformed from volatile to non-volatile characteristics through the application of external stimuli. Consequently, our device possesses both volatile and non-volatile memory characteristics, striving to perfectly mimic human cognition and response, thereby advancing the implementation of neuromorphic computing systems. This approach maximizes data processing efficiency and performance through the application of a novel neuromorphic computing architecture, enhancing high-performance computing and serving as a critical component in the development of artificial superintelligence.

The scientific world is witnessing an unprecedented triumph of artificial neural network (ANN)- a computing system inspired by the biological neural network. With the enthralling quest for Internet of Everything (IoE), it is expected to have an unparalleled dominance of ANN in our day-to-day life. In recent times, memristor has come as an emerging candidate to realize ANN through emulating biological synapse and neuron behavior. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), one well-known two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs), has drawn interest for high speed, flexible, low power electronic devices since it has a tunable bandgap, reasonable carrier mobility, high Young’s modulus, and large surface to volume ratio. Hence, in this work, 2D MoS2 based field effect transistor (FET) and memristor devices have been developed to evaluate the performance for advanced logic and neuromorphic computing applications. We probe the superior quality of 2D/high-κ dielectric interfaces by fabricating MoS2 based FET transistors with different gate dielectrics. This low interface trap density of ~7  10[10 ] states/cm[2] -eV at the MoS2/Al2O3 interface establishes the case for van der Waals systems where the superior quality of 2D/high-κ dielectric interfaces can produce high performance electronic and optoelectronic devices. This work also demonstrates Au/MoS2/Ag threshold switching memristor (TSM) device with low threshold voltage, sharp switching, high ON-OFF ratio and endurance. A leaky integration-and-firing (LIF) neuron is implemented with this TSM. It successfully emulates the key characteristics of a biological neuron. The LIF neuron is monolithically integrated with the MoS2 based synapse device to realize a single layer perceptron operation and Boolean logic gates. The Au/MoS2/Ag TSM device also imitates a nociceptor, the single device exhibits all the key features of nociceptors including threshold, relaxation, “no adaptation” and sensitization

iii

phenomena of allodynia and hyperalgesia. This work indicates applicability of this device in artificial intelligence systems-based neuromorphic hardware applications and artificial sensory alarm system.

An electronic synapse (e-synapse) based on memristive switching is a promising electronic element that emulates a biological synapse to realize neuromorphic computing. However, the complex resistive switching process it relies on hampers the reproducibility of its performance. Thus, achievement of a reproducible electronic synapse with a high rate of finished products has become a significant challenge in the development of an artificial intelligent circuit. Here, we demonstrate an ultrathin e-synapse having high yield (>95%), minimal performance variation, and extremely low power consumption based on an Al2O3/graphene quantum dots/Al2O3 sandwich structure that was fabricated using atomic layer deposition. The e-synapse showed both high device-to-device and cycle-to-cycle reproducibility with high stability, endurance, and switching uniformity, because the essential synaptic behaviors could be observed. This implementation of an e-synapse with an Al2O3/graphene quantum dots/Al2O3 structure should intensify motivation for engineering e-synapses for neuromorphic computing.

The forming current is limited to this range because for a compliance current below 50 nA, the filament formed is volatile (i.e., it resets the moment the forming is completed), whereas a compliance current higher than 150 nA leads to a filament which cannot be reset. The percentage of abrupt reset cases can be observed to increase with a decrease in the forming current compliance, from �64% at 150 nA to �86% at 50 nA. The results imply that the conductance plasticity of the HfO2 memristor may be lost when its dimension is scaled down. The loss would inevitably limit the scalability of memristors in synaptic applications, especially in spiking neural networks where analog weight tuning is mandatory for the functioning of the network.[30]
